item.title: Refuse the temptation to pass
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
This is an opinôÙion I someôÙtimes reôÙmemôÙber when seeôÙing some code. Don't take it as a strong adôÙvice, or a rule, but inôÙstead as a genôÙerôÙal guideôÙline that might help you to imôÙprove the code slightôÙly.

On this post I will share a tiny and opinôÙionôÙatôÙed arôÙguôÙment about why there are usuôÙalôÙly betôÙter things to do in the code inôÙstead of just pass.

Leer mûÀsãÎ (quedan 1 minutos de lectura)

item.title: Beyond coverage
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
It looks like there are a lot of opinôÙions or asôÙsumpôÙtions about unit tests and code covôÙerôÙage, most of them conôÙfusôÙing of biôÙased in sevôÙerôÙal ways. For exôÙamôÙple, I've heard or read things like "this is fine, it has X% covôÙerôÙage", or "checkôÙing for covôÙerôÙage on pull reôÙquests doesôÙn't help", or "dropôÙping the covôÙerôÙage levôÙel is not an isôÙsue", and many more of the like.

This arôÙtiôÙcle aims to shed some light on the isôÙsue of unit testôÙing with and code covôÙerôÙage. HopeôÙfulôÙly by the end of it, we'll get an idea of which of the preôÙviôÙous stateôÙments are right and which are wrong (spoilôÙer alert: they're all wrong, but for difôÙferôÙent reaôÙsonôÙs).

Leer mûÀsãÎ (quedan 5 minutos de lectura)

item.title: A few ideas on code review
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
AfôÙter watchôÙing a talk about code reôÙview 1, from the last EuôÙroPyôÙthon, I got some ideas that I'd like to sumôÙmarise.

Leer mûÀsãÎ (quedan 2 minutos de lectura)

item.title: Upcoming talk at EuroPython 2016
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
I am glad to inôÙform that I will be speakôÙing at EuôÙroPyôÙthon 2016 conôÙferôÙence.

I'll speak @euôÙropyôÙthon 2016:
'Clean code in Python' httpôÙs://t.ôÙco/e4ôÙclWU3aDM

ã MarôÙiôÙano (@rôÙmarôÙiôÙanoa) March 27, 2016

My subôÙmisôÙsion about clean code in Python was acôÙceptôÙed, so in the next ediôÙtion of EuôÙroPyôÙthon 2016, in BilôÙbao, Spain, I will talk about clean code prinôÙciôÙples for Python. Here is the abôÙstracôÙt:

httpôÙs://ep2016.euôÙropyôÙthon.euôÙ/ôÙconôÙferôÙence/talkôÙs/ôÙclean-ôÙcode-in-python

The full list of talks is availôÙable at:

httpôÙs://ep2016.euôÙropyôÙthon.euôÙ/en/events/sesôÙsionôÙs/

If you are inôÙterôÙestôÙed, subôÙscribe to the EuôÙroPyôÙthon blog and Youtube chanôÙnel. I will inôÙclude more deôÙtails in a sepôÙaôÙrate post.

item.title: Deleting commented out code
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
This is a rule I alôÙways enôÙcourôÙage in softôÙware deôÙvelôÙopôÙmenôÙt. MoreôÙover, I conôÙsidôÙer it to be someôÙthing that has to be inôÙcludôÙed in evôÙery codôÙing guideôÙline of a good projecôÙt.

There are sevôÙerôÙal reaôÙsons for this, but probôÙaôÙbly the best exôÙplaôÙnaôÙtion can be found in the book "Clean Code"1 by unôÙcle Bob, on which exôÙplains that the code, gets outôÙdatôÙed (rotôÙten) with the rest of the surôÙroundôÙing code, and hence it makes a place for conôÙfuôÙsion, leadôÙing to an erôÙror-prone spot.

There are, howôÙevôÙer, peoôÙple that seem to find some arôÙguôÙments for comôÙmentôÙing out code, or leavôÙing it. Some comôÙmon arôÙguôÙments/reaôÙsons usuôÙalôÙly are:

  • "I might need this funcôÙôÙtionôÙalôÙiôÙôÙty latôÙer.."

We have source conôÙtrol sysôÙtems (for exôÙamôÙple git) for this. In git, anyôÙthing can be reôÙstored from a preôÙviôÙous point. If the softôÙware is propôÙerôÙly unôÙder verôÙsion conôÙtrol, there is no reaôÙson to fear daôÙta loss. Trust git, code fearôÙlessôÙly.

  • "This is temôÙpoôÙrary disôÙôÙabled... It will be reôÙôÙsôÙtored latôÙer".

Again, same prinôÙciôÙple, reôÙly on the verôÙsion conôÙtrol sysôÙtem. Save a patch, and then reôÙstore latôÙer, or stash the changes, reôÙvert the comôÙmit, etc. As you see, there are plenôÙty of betôÙter opôÙtions for solvôÙing this sceôÙnariôÙo.

  • Code that was left from the fist verôÙôÙsion

ProbôÙaôÙbly deôÙbugôÙging leftôÙoverôÙs. No doubt here: seek, loôÙcate, deôÙstroy.

There is, a clear probôÙlem with code that is unôÙder comôÙmenôÙt, which is that it is "frozen" in time: it was good at some point, but then it was left there while the rest of the code around it, evolved, so this old code might not cerôÙtainôÙly work (hence it is "rotôÙten"), so un-ôÙcomôÙmentôÙing it is a bad idea beôÙcause it will probôÙaôÙbly crash.

AnôÙothôÙer probôÙlem is that it can be a source of bias for some othôÙer deôÙvelôÙopôÙer, who wants to mainôÙtain that code at a fuôÙture point in time. The one who left the rotôÙten code, might have thought that it was a source of inôÙspiôÙraôÙtion for when this funcôÙtionôÙalôÙiôÙty was goôÙing to be apôÙplied, but inôÙstead, it is just biôÙasôÙing the new deôÙvelôÙopôÙer with this skeleôÙton, preôÙventôÙing from a brand new, fresh idea for that code.

ThereôÙfore, for these main reaôÙsons (an probôÙaôÙbly much more), havôÙing code that is comôÙmentôÙed in a code base, is a poor pracôÙtice, (not to menôÙtion a code smell). If you are a seaôÙsoned deôÙvelôÙopôÙer, who cares about code qualôÙiôÙty, and best pracôÙtices, you must not doubt when deletôÙing it. Delete comôÙmentôÙed out code merôÙciôÙlessôÙly: seek, loôÙcate and deôÙstroy.

1

A book I highôÙly recôÙomôÙmend: httpôÙs://www.aôÙmaôÙzon.ôÙcom/ôÙClean-ôÙCode-HandôÙbook-ôÙSoftôÙware-CraftsôÙmanôÙship/dôÙp/0132350882

item.title: strncpy and strncat for copying strings in C
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
Recently, I've read an interesting article 1, explaining why the strncpy function in C is not safer than strcpy. The post was very interesting, but what's more, it suggested an alternative idiom for copying strings in C, that might probably be the way to go.

LatôÙer, in anôÙothôÙer arôÙtiôÙcle 2 that comôÙpared some funcôÙtionôÙalôÙiôÙty in C, Python and Go, one of the comôÙments pointôÙed out that very same idôÙiom. That grabbed my atôÙtenôÙtion, so I deôÙcidôÙed to try it in an exôÙamôÙple.

The problem with strncpy seems to be the way it manages the source string to be copied. Based on the sample code provided in the documentation 3 (that should be just a reference), the break condition is up to n characters (the third parameter) or until the source string is exhausted, whatever happens first. This should not be a problem, unless n < strlen(source_string). That parameter would make strncpy to finish before it can put a \0 character at the end of the target string, leaving an invalid array of characters 5.

This is an exôÙamôÙple.

#inôÙclude <stôÙdio.h>ô ô /* stdôÙout, fprintf */
#inôÙclude <string.h>ô ô /* strncpy, strlen */
#deôÙfine TARôÙGET 10
int main(int argc, char* argv[]) {
ô ô ô ô char *src = "Castle"; ô ô  /* 6 chars long */
ô ô ô ô char dst[TARôÙGET] = "__________";
ô ô ô ô dst[TARôÙGET - 1] = '\0';
ô ô ô ô fprintf(stdôÙout, "%s\n", dst); ô ô ô  /* must be: '_________' */
ô ô ô ô /* What hapôÙpens if I pass a wrong length (lowôÙer than the acôÙtuôÙal
ô ô ô ô * strôÙlen) */
ô ô ô ô strncpy(dst, src, 3);
ô ô ô ô fprintf(stdôÙout, "%s\n", dst); ô ô ô  /* must be: 'Cas______' */
ô ô ô ô /* If I copy the string corôÙrectôÙly, by passôÙing the right
ô ô ô ô * length, then strôÙcôÙnôÙpy beôÙhaves as exôÙpectôÙed */
ô ô ô ô strncpy(dst, src, strlen(src) + 1);
ô ô ô ô fprintf(stdôÙout, "%s\n", dst); ô ô ô  /* must be: 'Castle' */
ô ô ô ô reôÙturn 0;
}

On this example, the target array is represented by the variable dst, and I used a fixed-length string, on purpose for the demonstration, simulating what would actually happen. I null-terminated it so the program can finish successfully, because otherwise the operations on it would not end until the delimiter is reached, and we cannot know when that will happen, considering what's in memory at that time. In addition, the unpredictable behaviour will lead to errors, and probably to memory corruption. The underscore, should be interpreted as slots: regions or reserved memory that are there, but empty.

The proposed idiom uses strncat (see 4), tricking the function by passing it an empty string as the first parameter, and then the actual string we need to copy. This call will render the same result, but without the previous side effect. Let's see an example:

#inôÙclude <stôÙdio.h>ô ô /* stdôÙout, fprintf */
#inôÙclude <string.h>ô ô /* strnôÙcat, strlen */
#deôÙfine TARôÙGET 10
int main(int argc, char* argv[]) {
ô ô ô ô char *src = "Castle"; ô ô  /* 6 chars long */
ô ô ô ô char dst[TARôÙGET] = "__________";
ô ô ô ô dst[TARôÙGET - 1] = '\0';
ô ô ô ô fprintf(stdôÙout, "%s\n", dst); ô ô ô  /* must be: '_________' */
ô ô ô ô /* PreôÙpare desôÙtiôÙnaôÙtion string */
ô ô ô ô dst[0] = '\0';
ô ô ô ô /* Copy with strnôÙcat */
ô ô ô ô strnôÙcat(dst, src, 3);
ô ô ô ô fprintf(stdôÙout, "%s\n", dst); ô ô ô  /* must be: 'Cas' */
ô ô ô ô /* If I copy the string corôÙrectôÙly, by passôÙing the right
ô ô ô ô * length, then strôÙcôÙnôÙpy beôÙhaves as exôÙpectôÙed */
ô ô ô ô dst[0] = '\0';
ô ô ô ô strnôÙcat(dst, src, strlen(src) + 1);
ô ô ô ô fprintf(stdôÙout, "%s\n", dst); ô ô ô  /* must be: 'Castle' */
ô ô ô ô /* If I try to overôÙrun the bufôÙfer */
ô ô ô ô dst[0] = '\0';
ô ô ô ô strnôÙcat(dst, src, strlen(src) + 10);
ô ô ô ô fprintf(stdôÙout, "%s\n", dst); ô ô ô  /* must be: 'Castle' */
ô ô ô ô reôÙturn 0;
}

Here we see, the erôÙror is no longer preôÙsenôÙt, probôÙaôÙbly beôÙcause of the difôÙferôÙence on the imôÙpleôÙmenôÙtaôÙtion (the snipôÙpet on the docôÙuôÙmenôÙtaôÙtion 4 gives us a hint on what it does, so we can spot the change).

This might seem as a little issue, but it raised some concerns on the Linux kernel development, at the point that a new function was developed. The strscpy function is being included in the Kernel development for Linux 4.3-rc4 6 because it is a better interface. Some of the problems mentioned in the commit message, that inspired this new version, are the ones described on the previous paragraphs.

This makes me wonder, if this should be the "correct" way for performing this operation "safely" in C. In all cases, the error is the same (not checking the boundaries, and trusting the input), and should be avoided. What I mean by this, is that we cannot simply rely on those functions being secure, the security must be in our code, so the proper way to handle these situations is to code defensively: do not trust user input, always check the boundaries, error codes, memory allocation, status of the pointer (a free for every malloc but not for a NULL pointer, etc.).

1

httpôÙs://the-flat-ôÙtranôÙtor-ôÙsoôÙciôÙety.blogspot.ôÙcom.ar/2012/03/no-strncpy-is-not-saferôÙ-strôÙcpy.html.

2

httpôÙs://blog.ôÙsurgut.ôÙco.uk/2015/08/ôÙgo-enôÙjoy-python3.html.

3

strncpy docôÙuôÙmenôÙtaôÙtion.

4(1,2)

strnôÙcat manôÙuôÙal page.

5

An arôÙray of charôÙacôÙters that is not nulôÙl-terôÙmiôÙnatôÙed, is inôÙvalid.

6

httpôÙs://git.kôÙerôÙnel.org/cgit/linôÙux/kôÙerôÙnel/git/ôÙtorôÙvaldôÙs/linôÙux.git/ôÙcomôÙmit/?id=30c44659f4a3e7e1f9f47e895591b4b40bf62671.

item.title: Do not import *
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
It is well-known among Python developers (or at least, it should be), that is a bad idea to import everything from a module, for example by doing from <module> import *.

The idea on this post, is to highôÙlight and gathôÙer the reaôÙsons why this is a bad pracôÙtice, in orôÙder to colôÙlecôÙtiveôÙly idenôÙtiôÙfy many unôÙdeôÙsired efôÙfectôÙs. HowôÙevôÙer, withôÙout losôÙing pragôÙmaôÙtism, in case there are some odd reaôÙsons why this might be acôÙceptôÙable, I will alôÙso menôÙtion them, if any. Let's see where we get.

  1. You do not know what you get

    An arôÙbiôÙtrary Python script may conôÙtain any code, and most of it will be exôÙeôÙcutôÙed when perôÙformôÙing the imôÙport * part (yôÙou canôÙnot reôÙly on how __ôÙname__ is hanôÙdled). The inôÙterôÙface is toôÙtalôÙly unôÙclear: you do not know what comôÙpuôÙtaôÙtions perôÙformôÙs, what obôÙjects will imôÙport, etc. In genôÙerôÙal is more efôÙfiôÙcient to imôÙport as few defôÙiôÙniôÙtions as posôÙsiôÙble.

  2. IdenôÙtiôÙfiers apôÙpear magôÙiôÙcalôÙly

    In any deôÙcentôÙly readôÙable Python scripôÙt, the proôÙgramôÙmer must be able to loôÙcate evôÙery defôÙiôÙniôÙtion, which means to idenôÙtiôÙfy where does evôÙery idenôÙtiôÙfiôÙer come from. For exôÙamôÙple, a variôÙable named x can eiôÙther be a paôÙramôÙeôÙter of the funcôÙtion in the curôÙrent scope, a variôÙable alôÙready deôÙfined (asôÙsigned), or a name alôÙready imôÙportôÙed (from mod imôÙport x), etc. By perôÙformôÙing the inôÙcorôÙrect imôÙport, this variôÙable might apôÙpear out of the blue, meanôÙing that I will have an x that will not be neiôÙther a paôÙramôÙeôÙter, nor a defôÙiôÙniôÙtion nor a deôÙclared imôÙport. This meanôÙs, I canôÙnot track the oriôÙgin or x. The sitôÙuôÙaôÙtion gets worse if there are not one, but many imôÙport * stateôÙments. DeôÙbugôÙging beôÙcomes a nightôÙmare.

  3. NamesôÙpaces are one honkôÙing great idea -- let's do more of those!

    Straight from the Python zen 1. By imôÙôÙportôÙing evôÙeryôÙthing from a modôÙôÙule, the benôÙeôÙôÙfits of the namesôÙôÙpaces are someôÙhow lost. InôÙôÙstead, evôÙeryôÙthing (or a lot of things), might get to be called the same, messôÙing with the curôÙrent scope. MoreôÙover, new imôÙôÙport deôÙfôÙiôÙniôÙôÙtions might overôÙride preôÙviôÙous ones.

  4. ExôÙplicôÙit is betôÙter than imôÙplicôÙit

    Again, evôÙery idenôÙtiôÙfiôÙer that we want imôÙportôÙed should be done exôÙplicôÙitôÙly (the * is not very declarôÙaôÙtive).

Now, so far these might be some of the main reaôÙsons about why imôÙportôÙing evôÙeryôÙthing from a Python modôÙule is usuôÙalôÙly not a good idea. HowôÙevôÙer, in case the code at stake is just a simôÙple testôÙing scripôÙt, or an inôÙ-ôÙline senôÙtence on ipython, there could be nothôÙing wrong about it.

In adôÙdiôÙtion, alôÙthough I am not a big fan of imôÙport stateôÙments inôÙside funcôÙtions (someôÙtimes they are necôÙesôÙsary, though), imôÙportôÙing evôÙeryôÙthing from a packôÙage withôÙin a funcôÙtion is not a big probôÙlem, beôÙcause the scope is alôÙready narôÙrowed.

Just to be clear, this is by no means an abôÙsoôÙlute stateôÙmenôÙt, but an idea preôÙsentôÙed in orôÙder to write betôÙter code. One of the things I like the most about Python is that enôÙcourôÙages good pracôÙtices. ThereôÙfore, if I read an imôÙport stateôÙment like this, unôÙless there are some very good reaôÙsons to do so, I will think that line as a code-sôÙmell 2.

1

imôÙport this

2

httpôÙs://c2.ôÙcom/cgi/wikôÙi?ôÙCodeSmell

item.title: Default arguments in Python functions
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
This post is based on a gist I wrote a while ago, about why is not a good idea to pass deôÙfault muôÙtaôÙble obôÙjects as paôÙramôÙeôÙters in python funcôÙtion defôÙiôÙniôÙtionôÙs.

While the gist is exôÙplained through an exôÙamôÙple that usôÙes listôÙs, the prinôÙciôÙple is apôÙpliôÙcaôÙble to all sorts of obôÙjects (dicôÙtionarôÙies, setôÙs, etc.).

If you are an exôÙpeôÙriôÙenced python deôÙvelôÙopôÙer, you probôÙaôÙbly knew this caveat, nevôÙerôÙtheôÙless is someôÙthing inôÙterôÙestôÙing to show to new python deôÙvelôÙopôÙerôÙs, and to reôÙmemôÙber even if you have been writôÙing code in Python for years.

item.title: On returning consistent data types
item.dc:creator: Mariano Anaya
item.description:
This post is inôÙspired on an isôÙsue I once found, while I was usôÙing a well-ôÙknown liôÙbrary in Python for parsôÙing YAML files. The probôÙlem was that when it was loadôÙing the conôÙtent of the file, the reôÙsult was not coôÙherôÙenôÙt, beôÙcause someôÙtimes it reôÙturned the conôÙtent as a python dict, but if the file was empôÙty, the reôÙturn valôÙue was None.

Do you noôÙtice someôÙthing odd here?

What if I want to use the reôÙsult? I canôÙnot do it safeôÙly, for exôÙamôÙple:

conôÙtent = yaml.load(...) ô ô  # with the corôÙrect paôÙramôÙeôÙters and file name
for tag, valôÙues in conôÙtent.items():
ô ô ô ô pass ô ô  # process as reôÙquired...

If content is None, it will raise an AttributeError saying that None has no attribute called "items" (which is true).

ThereôÙfore, the deôÙvelôÙopôÙer should catch the exôÙcepôÙtion or avoid the corôÙner case, by doôÙing someôÙthing like the folôÙlowôÙing:

content = yaml.load() or {}

That could be a case of "coding defensively", making sure that the program will not fail under most conditions (it would also require to add an assert or to raise an exception perhaps, but that is a different topic). I actually agree with defensive programming, but I think it is better if the library itself has a more correct behaviour, respecting the interface (that is: if you are going to return a dictionary, and there is not content, then the logical assumption is to expect an empty dictionary). This must be the default behaviour, not something to be set by parameters.

This could be thought as an inôÙstance of a more genôÙerôÙal probôÙlem that ocôÙcurs when some funcôÙtion is inôÙtendôÙed to reôÙturn "X or Y". In my opinôÙion, if X and Y do not share the same inôÙterôÙface, there is a poôÙtenôÙtial bug (in the ObôÙjecôÙt-OriôÙentôÙed parôÙaôÙdigm we would say that there is no polyôÙmorôÙphisôÙm, or maybe that the "conôÙtracôÙt" is not beôÙing reôÙspectôÙed).

This is an exôÙamôÙple that I wantôÙed to highôÙlight, beôÙcause it might help you to write cleanôÙer code.
